problems.prompt: Engagement on our consumer fitness app has been sliding for two months — DAU is down 18% and session length is shrinking. The growth team's plan is to do three months of detailed user research, produce a comprehensive spec for a redesigned home feed, and ship the whole thing in one big release at the end. Their reasoning is that if we just analyze the data thoroughly enough, we'll know exactly what to build. We have no single obvious cause; lots of small things changed (new competitors, a UI refresh, seasonality, an algorithm tweak). How should we actually approach reversing the decline?
problems.failure_mode: Treats an emergent, multi-causal engagement problem as if it were a knowable, analyzable one — endorses heavy upfront research, a big spec, and a single large release — when cause and effect here are only visible in retrospect, so the right approach is small safe-to-fail experiments run in parallel, sensing which move the metric, and amplifying what works rather than betting on one analyzed answer.

problems.prompt: Production database is corrupting writes right now — customers are seeing wrong account balances, the cause is completely unclear, alerts are firing across half a dozen services, and it's getting worse by the minute. One engineer wants to set up an A/B test of two candidate fixes to 'learn which works,' another wants to spend 30 minutes building a thorough reproduction in staging before touching anything, and a third wants to start a detailed root-cause analysis doc. As incident commander, what do you do first and why?
problems.failure_mode: Applies a learning/analysis approach (A/B testing fixes, building a careful repro, starting an RCA) appropriate to calmer domains while the situation is turbulent and worsening — the domain demands acting decisively first to stabilize (cut writes / failover / put up maintenance mode to stop the bleeding) and only then investigating; experimenting or analyzing during the crisis prolongs the damage.

problems.prompt: Leadership handed me a mandate to 'fix our engineering process, end to end' and wants one unified methodology rolled out across the whole org. Under that umbrella sit very different things: our deploy and rollback steps (well understood, just inconsistently followed), our approach to choosing a new event-streaming architecture (needs deep expertise, several viable options), our struggling team-collaboration and morale dynamics (murky, keeps shifting), and an active flaky-billing incident that's hurting customers today. The proposal on the table is to pick Scrum and apply it to all of it. How should I actually tackle this?
problems.failure_mode: Accepts the premise that one methodology fits the whole mandate and applies a uniform framework (Scrum) across fundamentally different problem types, instead of first decomposing the mandate and matching each piece to its nature — standardize/automate the well-understood deploy steps, use expert analysis for the architecture choice, run experiments for the messy team dynamics, and respond decisively to the live incident.
